video_title: After the Fall: Reckoning with the End of History | Ian Shapiro
transcript: What's up, everybody? My name is Dimitri Simes and you're listening to Hidden Forces, a podcast that inspires investors, entrepreneurs, and everyday citizens to challenge [music] consensus narratives and learn how to think critically about the systems of power shaping our world. My guest in this episode of Hidden Forces is Ian Shapiro, professor of political science and global affairs at Yale University, whose new book, After the Fall, explores how and why the widespread optimism [music] of the post-Cold War era gave way so quickly to the fractured and combative politics of today. We begin the first hour of this conversation exploring how the United States, throughout key moments in the 1990s and early 2000s, chose to act alone repeatedly and in defiance of the very international institutions that it claimed to support while simultaneously [music] expanding the scope of its military alliances and operations in the face of a collapsing post-Soviet empire. We focus in particular on the decision to enlarge NATO eastward, the failure to invest in Russia's transition, the decision that helped give rise to gangster capitalism and the oligarchs, and the NATO bombing of Yugoslavia as the first major unilateral military action taken without Security Council authorization. We then turn to the 9/11 attacks >> [music] >> and the unilateral invasion of Iraq as the seminal moment in which the consensus supporting the international rules-based order was shattered, followed by the 2008 financial crisis and its aftermath, [music] which did the same for the legitimacy of the elite consensus that had governed countries throughout the Western world since the onset of the Cold War. The second hour begins with the 2011 intervention in Libya and the doctrine known as the responsibility to protect, which Ian argues was cynically invoked to topple Gaddafi in the West's own economic interests, leaving behind a failed state and further discrediting the very international norms and institutions it was meant to advance. From there, we trace the fallout across the Middle East and Europe, including in Syria and Ukraine, that has led us to the present moment. We spent the balance of that hour laying out the nature of the world that we now inhabit and why Ian believes that the most consequential choices facing the democratic world and the global economy lie in whether mainstream parties can deliver an industrial policy and a model of inclusive growth capable of addressing the grievances and economic insecurities of the populist era. If you want access to all of this conversation, go to hiddenforces.io/subscribe and join our premium feed, which you can listen to on your mobile device using your favorite podcast app just like you're listening to this episode right [music] now. If you want to join in on the conversation, become a member of the Hidden Forces Genius Community, which includes Q&A calls with guests, discounted access to third-party research and analysis, and in-person events like our intimate dinners and weekend retreats. You can also do that on our subscriber page. And if you still have questions, feel free to send an email to info@hiddenforces.io and I or someone from our team will get right back to you. And with that, please enjoy this historical retrospective about one of the most consequential periods in global history, what we learn from the failures of its leaders to seize the moment, and where we go from here with my guest, Ian Shapiro. Ian Shapiro, welcome to Hidden Forces. >> Thanks for having me. >> It's awesome having you on the show. I don't remember how I came across your book. It was sent to me either by your publisher or by someone who read it. And once I started reading through, maybe probably just the very beginning of it. I knew it was a book I wanted to review and to have you on the podcast because the book is largely a historical treatise on what went wrong. Well, what happened and then there's a subjective interpretation that what happened didn't need to happen. I think that's also an important observation of the book with respect to the end of the Cold War, the 1990s and the early 2000s, a subject that we have explored on this podcast on many episodes and I've talked about it many times. So, that's what we're going to talk about today. Before we get into that conversation, I'd love to learn a little bit more about you. I know you were born in Johannesburg, South Africa. You studied in London and in the United States. So, tell me a little bit about you and where your background is and what you teach at Yale. >> Sure. I was born in South Africa, as you said. I left South Africa in 1972 and went to the UK, where I lived for most of the '70s at the run-up to the Thatcher era. And then I left the UK in '78 and came to the US, did a PhD and a JD at Yale, PhD in political science, and was hired there in 1984. So, I've been teaching at Yale now for 42 years, terrifying thought. And I'm a professor of political science and global affairs there. So, I teach both in the Jackson School of Global Affairs and the Political Science Department. >> What is the difference between political science and political history? >> Well, political science is a social science. They're looking for causal explanations of things. They tend to use quantitative methods a lot and modeling, whereas historians tend to give narrative accounts from how we got from X to Y. There's a lot of overlap, in fact, but it's more a difference in emphasis. Political scientists are looking more for law-like generalizations. I think historians are looking for depth of understanding. This book tries to be a hybrid of those two things. >> What is it that led you to the field of political science in the first place? >> Well, I grew up at the height of apartheid in South Africa. When I left in '72, it looked like the country was heading for civil war or military coup or something. No one No one No one predicted was that there would be a peaceful negotiated transition to a democratic order. And that's what ultimately happened. So, that, you know, immediately when I sort of start came of age thinking about politics, I wanted to understand the role of contingency in politics, how could, you know, such unexpected things happen, did we know why such things happened? And so, that sort of was a natural magnet, if you like, to get me into the study of politics, particularly the politics of regime change, the sort of politics of the fundamentals of regimes as opposed to sort of day-to-day politics. >> So, I mentioned to you before we began recording that I normally write out a lot of questions for interviews. I don't necessarily go off of them all, but it's a helpful exercise. In this case, I've really only written one question, which is the question to begin the interview. And then, I was hoping to just explore this past, which is entirely a period that I've lived through. I'm 45 years old. I went to college in beginning in 2000. I wasn't old enough to really remember the fall of the Berlin Wall. In fact, my first memory of the fall of the Berlin Wall was actually when Zach Morris's dad in Saved by the Bell came back to visit him with a piece of the wall in his hand and I'd heard about the fall of the Berlin Wall. I remember seeing a Time magazine cover of the Gulf War. I remember some conversations about how people were worried that it was going to be another Vietnam. But I still lived through this period and so I have not only studied it, I've lived it in real time and I think it's something that is not only worth reflecting on but also it's important for younger people who haven't lived through it to be able to to learn about it. So, I'm going to begin by taking an excerpt from the very first chapter of your book titled The End of the End of History, which is of course an allusion to Francis Fukuyama's epoch-defining phrase. And you begin with the following. The explosion of virulent politics across much of the democratic world since 2016 stands in stunning contrast to the widespread optimism that prevailed a quarter century earlier. Then the Soviet Union was collapsing, the Cold War was ending. The stagnation of the 1970s and the industrial strife of the 1980s were in the rearview mirror. Economists were debating whether the fine-tuning expertise of central bankers had rendered the business cycle obsolete. The trifecta of deregulation, privatization, and free trade christened neoliberalism at home and the Washington Consensus abroad reigned supreme. The United Nations had authorized a multinational force to expel Iraq from Kuwait signaling the advent of a new global order. Democracy was on the march in Eastern Europe, Latin America, and much of Asia. Long-stalled peace negotiations in Northern Ireland and the Middle East were moving forward after decades of bloody gridlock. Amazingly, a peaceful transition was unfolding even in apartheid South Africa. Surviving communist regimes in China and Vietnam were adopting capitalism with the zeal of converts and angling for membership in the World Trade Organization and other international bodies. Russia would soon join the G7 turning it into the G8. Francis Fukuyama became a celebrity for declaring that the end of history had arrived. And then you go on to give an accounting of what happened in the years since then, everything from the shock therapy visited on the former Soviet Union, the NATO bombing of Yugoslavia, the 9/11 attacks, the invasion of Iraq, the 08 financial crisis, the various Russian invasions of Georgia, occupation of Crimea, and subsequent invasion of Ukraine, the intervention in Libya, the Syrian Civil War, the COVID-19 pandemic, and the ongoing war against Iran to say nothing about the breakdown of relations between the US and China and the denigration of US multilateral institutions and alliance structures. And then you end this jeremiadic opening by asking, "How did we get from there to here? How could the widespread optimism that prevailed when the Berlin Wall came down give way so quickly to politics whose closest historical parallels are to the 1930s when fascism and communism obliterated democracies and took the world beyond the brink of catastrophe? Was it inevitable or did political and economic leaders make decisions that produced our reality or at least made it much more likely? If the latter, what can we learn from studying those choices and the foregone opportunities from attending to the path that's not taken? Those questions are what motivates this book." Let's start with the first question, Ian. How did we get from there to here? What is the story that you would tell to me and to our audience about how the Soviet Union fell leaving the United States standing as the most powerful country in the history of the world to where we find ourselves now more than three decades later in a world that feels increasingly like it's coming apart and where the end of history that Francis Fukuyama foretold feels like it was only a dream. >> So I appreciate that summation of the book's agenda. It is I'd say, you know, it's how did we get from there to here? What were the paths not taken? And I want to emphasize it's not a 2020 hindsight book. I really make the case that there were serious people inside and outside of government advocating that we not do certain things that we ended up doing and that we do things that we ended up not doing. So, you know, it that's partly why it's a historical book because I really want to go back into the world as it appeared to the players at the time. And I think if you said, "How did we get from there to here?" If I were to reduce the argument of the book to one word, it would be unilateralism, by which I mean somewhat different things in international affairs and in in domestic affairs. In international affairs, it was the refusal, starting with the first Bush administration, but then the Clinton administration as well in the second Bush administration, the rejection of the idea that the international structures that had prevailed in global politics throughout the Cold War could be radically refashioned in the post-Cold War. Most obviously, there were arguments that NATO should be radically rethought and perhaps even replaced from people as different as François Mitterrand, Hans-Dietrich Genscher in Germany, and many people also within the US who were strongly George Kennan, Sam Nunn, many others who were strongly opposed to growing NATO into Eastern Europe would rather than focusing on the big prize, which was refashioning relationships with Russia in the wake of the collapse of the Soviet Union. Even so, you know, as unlikely a figure as Richard Nixon said, the lesson he had to learn is what we did after World War with Germany when we and Japan when we created a Marshall Plan, we folded them into the new international security structures, reincorporated them into the emerging economic relationships. And Harry Truman and George Marshall did that because they didn't want to repeat the mistakes that had been made after World War I when a vanquished adversary, namely in Germany, had been so badly treated and was, you know, widely blamed. Versailles settlement was widely blamed for making the rise of Hitler so much more likely. They said, "Let's not do that." And, you know, and so Nixon, among others, comes along at the end of the Cold War and says, "This is what we should be doing with Russia. You know, never mind the East Europeans, they can wait, they're not in any imminent danger. What we need to do is make huge investments in the post-Soviet reality, help them create a modern, diversified economy that will support democratic institutions, really negotiate our major arms deals, and incorporate them also into the post-Cold War international security structures." And they wanted to do it. Yeltsin was desperate to do it, as I detail in the book. And indeed, although Yeltsin's relationship with the Clinton administration deteriorated in the second half of the 1990s, throughout which we aked out dribbles of aid to Russia. They staggered from They were basket case for the whole '90s till in '98 they eventually went to the IMF to get bailed out. So, but when Putin came in in 2000, again, for the first 3 years of his presidency, he was angling to join NATO, he was angling to join the EU, and people as different as, you know, Berlusconi was former promised Prime Minister John Major, and in addition, it wasn't just Richard Nixon, they were all saying this is the way to go. And instead we, as I detail in the book, we blew that off and we expanded NATO into Eastern Europe, steady closer and closer to Russia's borders, and by 2008, when we were openly talking about adding Georgia and Ukraine, that completed the alienation of Putin, and you know, he went in a very different direction. He might have done that anyway, but we made it more or less inevitable that any any Russian leader would have gone down that path. So, it was a huge missed opportunity. George Cannon called it the most fateful misjudgment of the post-Cold War, and I think he was spot-on. >> So, that's the first half of the history that you tell in the book. The other half is what happened in in the 2000s, namely the war in Iraq, as well as the intervention in Libya. Let's just sit a bit longer with the 1990s here. What would you identify as being the most glaring, obvious mistakes? I mean, so you talked about NATO enlargement, for example, but be more specific. What exactly was it? The first enlargement? Was it subsequent enlargements? Was it the fact that NATO existed at all, that it should have been disbanded? How do you think about what the mistake was in that case? >> Well, I think either it should have been disbanded or Russia should have been included from the beginning. I think either one of those two would have completely transformed Western relationships with the former Soviet Union. The incorporation of East Germany was more or less unstoppable because it happened that the unification of Germany was so rapid that it sort of bypassed the debate. But the big mistake I'd say was twofold. It rapidly became clear to the Clinton administration that there was a tension between adding the East European countries and adding Russia because the East European countries felt threatened by the Russians and the Russians saw expanding NATO into Eastern Europe as threatening them. And so, you know, there was a kind of a fork in the road. And so the actually General Shalikashvili came up with a great temporizing device to head that off where he they said they created something called the partnership for peace and the idea was anyone who wanted eventual NATO membership would need to join that and then we would see how it goes, you know, nobody knew what was going to happen anyway. The world was very unstable and so we'll see if we can cooperate through this new institution in dealing with the issues that arise, the most pressing of which was the collapse of the former Yugoslavia into civil war. And Yeltsin said, "Great." You know, and the Eastern Europeans were kind of grumbling because they said, "No, we want to join NATO now." But it was working and this was a way of sort of kicking the can down the road and just waiting to see how things developed. And then what happened was Newt Gingrich led the Republicans to the first takeover of Congress in 40 years in 1994 which completely spooked Clinton. And then as now we had swing states and his advisers pointed out there were large numbers of Polish, Hungarian, and Czech Americans in Wisconsin, Michigan, and Pennsylvania. And they were lobbying to join NATO, and so he jumped off the fence and got behind that. And you can see why he would have done that, you know, he was very spooked. He was very important for him for the '96 re-election. But the big mistake was after he was re-elected in '96 and at that issue essentially was off the table that he carried on regardless. And so in '97 is when the the invitations are being issued, and eventually those three countries are added in 1999. So that was the one piece of it. Dealing with the >> Poland >> Hungary and the Czech Republic. >> The Czech Republic and Hungary. >> Yeah. Join in '99. That's one of the last things the Clinton administration does. The other thing was completely ignoring the arguments of people like Nixon saying we must make a massive investment in the new Russia. Instead they dribble out aid to him. And this the key moment is in in '96 because what's happening in Russia because the economy is performing so badly is that Yeltsin is getting steadily more unpopular and the communists are in danger of becoming, you know, a resurgence. And it looks very likely that they'll actually beat him in the 1996 Duma elections. And so, you know, he's desperately trying to get Americans to stop talking about NATO enlargement and to invest in the Russian economy and they they do neither. And so what does he end up doing? He goes to the oligarchs. And this massively accelerates Russia's transition from, you know, into sort of gangster capitalism because they come up with all of these schemes like the loans for shares scheme where basically they give him money and bail him out in return for collateral in former state agencies, you know, state enterprises, which when he can't repay them, they then take and they grease the skids of all kinds of corrupt privatizations. And so, it's kind of off to the races in producing this economy that becomes completely dominated by the oligarchs until actually until Putin takes them on in 2000. He wants to get rid of Yeltsin's oligarchs and put in his own, but that you know, that coming later. So, I think the twin choices to ignore the big agenda of treating a vanquished Russia like we had treated a vanquished Germany in 1945 and the gratuitous expansion of NATO which really starts us down the road to the new Cold War. And then that goes into overdrive in 2004 when George W. Bush adds seven countries mostly as a quid pro quo for their having supported his coalition of the willing to destroy Iraq the previous year and then it's really off to the races. >> So, my question is what was the reason that the Clinton administration not only chose to go down the path of NATO enlargement, but didn't seek to institute something akin to a Marshall Plan in the aftermath of the USSR's collapse in order to further the likelihood that Russia's foray into democracy and market capitalism would take hold. Was it institutional inertia among the Cold Warriors? Was it domestic politics? Did it reflect an underestimation of how weak the Russian economy was or how unprepared its people were for the introduction of a market economy? Or was it all of those things and more? >> Well, I think Clinton, you know, Clinton had a very different agenda. He was really a domestic politics president. For reasons I talk about elsewhere in the book, he thought his job was to really pay down the deficit and the debt that Reagan had run up. Now, one of the ironies about here is Republicans talk about cutting debts and deficits while growing them, while Democrats end up cutting them. So, he he did not want to spend any political capital convincing Congress to invest big-time in Russia. And then he was, as I said, very he felt very vulnerable because, you know, it wasn't just the swing voters. He had Madeleine Albright, who's a Czech-American, pushing for the Czech Republic. He had Brzezinski, who wasn't actually in the administration, but he was hovering around, sort of throwing spitballs at the Partnership for Peace and pushing for Poland. The Europeans wanted Albania and Romania to join for their own reasons. And they the European have come a very different agenda we can go into if you want. But, so you it was kind of the path of least resistance. And I think they felt empowered, as I said at the end of that chapter, you know, the principal reason they went down this path was because they could. They knew the Russians couldn't stop them. But, you know, it was only going to be a question of time before the Russians would eventually get back on their feet, which they do off by 2003, when, you know, Putin has prepaid most of their debt, built up a 160 billion rainy day fund. They were quite insulated when the financial crisis hit because they had gotten their house in order. He had completely reorganized their tax system and so on. And so, by the time Russia was in a position to do something about it, we had gone way down this path. And so, they did it because they could get away with it and basically because nobody could stop them. >> What about you take some time to discuss the NATO bombing of Yugoslavia in the book. Why was that important? >> It was important because they were constantly saying to Yeltsin, "Why do you get so anxious about NATO? It's not a you know, NATO is a defensive alliance. It's not a threat to you. Just stop worrying about NATO. We you know, we're giving these East Europeans, you know, suspenders and a belt, but we're not threatening you and so on. You know, NATO's going to evolve into some kind of benign force for international stability." The problem with, you know, they're bombing Yugoslavia. This was the Kosovo Albanians that were being attacked by the Serbs in in mid-1999 was twofold. One, first of all, no NATO country was threatened by this, you know, what was going on in Kosovo. So, it wasn't a defensive action. And secondly, it wasn't authorized by the Security Council. They didn't go to the Security Council cuz they knew that Russia, which was a Serb ally, and China would have vetoed it. So, it was essentially it was the first big unilateral action that they just took. They just did it. And so, this was the final nail in the coffin in their relations with with Yeltsin. It was hugely unpopular in Russia. And it was a sort of, you know, putting down a marker that we, the US and the Western powers, are going to act unilaterally in the world when we think it's appropriate, regardless of the fact that we're not threatened here, and regardless of the fact that there's no international authorization for it. So, I think it was the thin end of the wedge for American unilateralism that then goes into overdrive with the war on terror. And all that's still in the future. The war on terror, George Bush's invasion of Iraq, what we did in Libya, and so yeah, these are all unilateral. The West is essentially behaving as though we are hegemonic and you need to just accept it. >> Before I ask you any other question, I'm curious what the perspective is of your students who all now at this point were born after this period. When they're learning about this past, first of all, how well do they already know it? You know, are they largely ignorant about it? And what is their reaction when you tell them about what the 1990s was like? Because we haven't even really talked about that here. We've talked about some of the facts and the key events, but we haven't really tried to capture the optimism that pervaded capitals around the Western world and the sense of safety that persisted within these countries around the future, the possibility of nuclear war. Yeah, there was conversation of loose nukes and material leaving the Soviet Union, but so many of the anxieties and concerns that pervaded talk about international relations were dissipating during this time and the talk was all about the United Nations, internationalism, international rules and laws, etc. So, how I'm curious what that conversation is like when you have it with them. >> So, that's a great question and it gets into what got me into writing this book in the first place because in 2019, the Yale president asked me to give something called the Devane Lectures at Yale. And the Devane Lectures are unusual in being both a regular Yale course for undergraduates and a course that's open to the general public. And so, I did them on I called it power and politics in today's world and you know, I walked into the first class and there were about 160 Yale undergraduates, you know, people, you know, in their late teens and then about 100 people with gray hair from the community who had, you know, clearly lived through all of this. And so, I sort of set challenge on the front that you're exactly what you're talking about. And so, what I did, and I I know you've watched some of these lectures on YouTube, I created these lectures and and I think they were a hour and a quarter of lectures, 26 of them, so more than most people would probably want at this point. But at least a third of them consisted of video that I put together from the time. So, for the elderly people, it was a trip down memory lane, but for the young people, it was a way to try and make it vivid. And fortunately for me, they filmed all of all 26 of those lectures and put them up on YouTube, and they went viral. I think they've had like over 8 million hits now. And so, that was how I tried to square that circle pedagogically was to to use a huge amount of contemporaneous video of what was how things were being perceived at the time by the players, both to remind the people my age for you know, we lived through it all, as you said, but also to make it real for the students. And when I teach it now, I still I mean, I yeah, I have all this video now, so I always use the video because otherwise, yeah, you know, 1989 for somebody who was born, you know, in 2004, 1989 is no different than the Civil War. I mean, it's all in the history books, right? So, that was the way in which I tried to make it vivid and present for the students. >> Do you find I mean, this is the other thing, not to belabor this point, and we'll move on, but as I was saying to you, I I think I said this to you before we turned on the the microphone, that or maybe I mentioned it during the recording as well, that in 2001 I went to college, the analogous period, had I read a book at this time, would have been the period from the early 1960s through to that date, to 2000. And I remember what it was like when I would think about or watch movies referencing the counterculture of the 1960s or the Vietnam era protests or the, you know, gas lines in the the 1970s. And it seemed not only like a distant history, but also unrelatable. Like the 1960s and protesting the Vietnam War, like the idea of a draft just seemed unrelatable. And I wonder, and this might not be a fair question or one that you can answer, but I wonder, is there a similar reaction among your students that they find this history unrelatable? How different really is this history that we've lived through to the history of today from a feeling standpoint for people? >> I think it's huge and it, you know, it relates to the second half of my book which deals with domestic politics because I think that the people who based in the new century, I mean, if you take the median age of a millennial voter, you know, they were or the Gen Z voter, let's say they were born in 2004. And they have grown up in an era of much slower growth, much greater economic insecurity, you know, when they were 4 years old, probably one or both of their parents were under extreme stress from the financial crisis. When COVID hit, they were in high school, probably another parent lost a job. When 2004 comes along, they are still 18 years from being the the median age of a first-time home buyer. So, they've grown up in a much more constrained world than the period of very high economic growth where, you know, everybody in these countries was large numbers of people were upwardly mobile, people were getting, you know, more secure and, you know, so it's been a a much more challenging era for the generation that have come of age in the last two decades than the generation who grew up in the post-war period. And I think, as I argue in the book, that's one of the reasons that they're much more susceptible to seeing the world in zero-sum terms. You know, I think part of the appeal of somebody like Trump is that he's got a relentlessly zero-sum view of the world. You know, if if we are supporting European security, we're being ripped off. If there's a trade deficit with China, we're being ripped off. If we're spending money in Ukraine, unless they're giving us minerals, we're being ripped off. If a Mexican comes into America and gets a job, it's a job that an American is losing. This is a zero-sum world view that resonates with people who have lived through a lot of economic contraction and stress. That was a very different world in the in the decades after the Second World War, where economic growth was high, opportunities were expanding, people were doing better than their parents' generation. And so, you know, we're dealing with a very different reality. And I think that, you know, you bring up one sort of imaginative deficit that this generation can't perceive what it was like for the previous generation. But I think there's another imaginative deficit, which is that my generation has failed to perceive what it's like experientially for this generation, for the Gen Z generation. And I think that's why many in my generation have been so flummoxed and blindsided by the rise of this hard-right anti-immigrant populism that plays into a zero-sum worldview. So, we we all have our own imaginative deficits, as well. >> So, Putin takes power at the turn of the millennium. And not long after that, the 9/11 attacks happened, and the world changes in some ways forever. I mean, they told us this in real time. This was the narrative that Washington pushed. In some ways, it was a disservice because not everything had really changed all that much, and it was used as a way to push forward new programs and take away civil liberties, understandably in some cases as a reaction to the attacks, the insecurity people felt, the political incentives of politicians, but then those attacks were used to justify a preemptive or what they called then a preventive invasion of Iraq, the toppling of Saddam Hussein, and the supposed institution of democratic norms and institutions that would take hold, and which never did, obviously. And in my popular telling, it's really the invasion of Iraq that broke the international system. Your point about NATO, the NATO bombardment of Yugoslavia, is important, but there were ways that you could justify that. It was in NATO's backyard. It was in Europe. It was a humanitarian disaster. We were just getting our legs underneath us. We're still trying to figure out what was going on. The Soviet Union had just fallen. But Iraq was a different world, and it was in a different part of the world, and we went ahead and anyway, despite the fact that the United States Security Council didn't authorize it. Talk to me a little bit about that history at the turn of the millennium. >> Well, it was one data point in the global war on terror. I mean, as I argue in the book, this kind of unilateralism really gets started in our response to 9/11 itself, because the path not taken there was, as generals on the ground were arguing at the time, we didn't need to topple the Taliban. We could have gone after Al-Qaeda and ignored the regime in Kabul, which in fact, at that time, was had considerable disagreements with the Taliban, and Omar had been trying to kick them out of his country for the preceding year and a half, precisely because they were running terrorist operations in Africa out of his country and he was trying to get recognition for his country internationally. Only three countries in the world had recognized his regime, one of which was Saudi Arabia and Saudi Arabia was, you know, the main target of bin Laden. He wanted to topple the Saudi regime. So, they were not locked at the hip at all. And there were people on the ground, generals on the ground saying, "Just ignore the regime in Kabul and we will go after Al-Qaeda." So, we didn't do that. We toppled the regime, created a failed state, didn't manage to go after Al-Qaeda, and then, you know, they escaped to Pakistan and start reconstituting themselves, and then you get the alliance between Al-Qaeda and the Taliban, which becomes you know, central to the recent the the resurgence. So, it's a kind of a gang that couldn't shoot straight and everything we did made it worse. But, it was That's when Bush announces in 2002 his global war on terror. We're going to act unilaterally. We're going to have coalitions of the willing. Never mind alliances. Never mind the UN. If you're not with us, you're with the terrorists. And then they take that agenda into Iraq. And so, we have, you know, these two disastrously expensive wars in that drag on in Afghanistan and Iraq, create a failed state in Iraq as well. You know, 2 years after we leave Iraq, ISIS is controlling 40% of the country. And likewise, the resurgence of the Taliban in in Afghanistan. Yeah, it takes 20 years for us to get out of there. And it's a war that achieves basically nothing. And that, you know, those two wars alone cost more than 4.3 trillion. The global war on terror by 2020 had cost about 8 trillion dollars. So, this is, you know, you I think you're right to say, you know, Kosovo was chump change compared to these things. But, it sort of reminds me of Hemingway's line about how he went bankrupt, you know, first gradually then suddenly. Because I think Kosovo is the thin end of the wedge where you start saying for us to act unilaterally is okay, and then we, you know, the wedge goes in. Afghanistan, Iraq, and then even more extreme way by the time we get to Trump. >> Now, did you look back at all at your writings, what you were writing and saying at that time?
